A scientist designed an experiment to test the effect of temperature on bacterial growth. He grew three different cultures of E. coli under three heat lamps at different temperatures. What was the independent variable?
explanation: The scientist deliberately CHANGED the temperature (three different temperatures). Temperature is what was manipulated → Independent Variable. Number of bacteria and reproduction rate are results that would be measured (DV). Length of the experiment is a constant.

stem: A student wanted to look at plant growth in five different soil samples. He planted the same type of seeds in identical containers in full sunlight and gave each the same amount of water. What is the independent variable?
explanation: The student used FIVE DIFFERENT soil samples — that is what was deliberately changed between groups. Seeds, containers, sunlight, and water were all kept the same (constants). Plant stem growth is the DV (what was measured). Remember: the IV is the ONE thing that changes.

stem: Two plant species in a dry region: Species A has slow stem growth and few leaves but is very abundant. Species B has rapid stem growth and many leaves but is very rare. Which hypothesis is most likely supported?

options.a: Flower size and color may give species B an advantage over species A.
options.b: Reduced root growth may give species A an advantage over species B.
options.c: Leaf shape may give species B an advantage over species A.
options.d: Reduced stem growth may give species A an advantage over species B.

explanation: In a DRY environment, slow growth and few leaves mean LESS water loss through transpiration. Species A is abundant BECAUSE its traits conserve water. The hypothesis must connect the observable trait (reduced stem growth) to the survival outcome. Flower size, root growth, and leaf shape were not mentioned in the data — only stem growth and leaf count were.

stem: A student's experiment showed that earthworms move away from light. This statement should be classified as —
explanation: A conclusion is a statement summarizing the results AFTER an experiment is completed. The student ran the experiment, observed the earthworms moving away from light, and concluded that behavior. It is not a hypothesis (that comes before the experiment), not a prediction (that is about the future), and not an inference (that requires interpretation beyond the data). The experiment directly showed the result — making it a conclusion.

stem: Data about the climate in an ecosystem were collected for 30 years. Which hypothesis about a population of eagles could be made BASED ON the climate data collected?

options.a: Eagle chick survival is directly related to annual rainfall amounts.
options.b: An outbreak of disease in 1987 killed 82% of the eagle population.
options.c: Eagles remain with the same mate throughout their lifetime.
options.d: Mortality of eagles from pesticides after 1992 was less than 5%.

explanation: A hypothesis must be BASED ON the data collected. Since CLIMATE data was collected (which includes rainfall, temperature, etc.), only a hypothesis relating eagles to climate factors makes sense. Disease outbreaks, mating behavior, and pesticide mortality are not climate data — they cannot be hypothesized from climate observations alone.

stem: In an experiment, the height of several plants was recorded daily in millimeters. Which tool would be the most accurate and appropriate for this measurement?
explanation: Plant height is a LENGTH measurement in MILLIMETERS. A metric ruler measures length in mm. A graduated cylinder measures liquid volume, a digital scale measures mass, and a yardstick uses inches/feet (not metric). Always match the TOOL to the TYPE of measurement and UNIT required.

stem: A biology class of 24 students measures the height of each student and calculates the average. Which is a possible source of error?

options.a: The number of males and females in the class
options.b: The difference in the ages of the students
options.c: The total number of students in the class
options.d: The accuracy of making and recording measurements

explanation: Measurement error comes from HOW you take and record measurements. If students measure incorrectly (bad posture, rounding errors, misreading the tape), the data will be wrong. The other options describe characteristics of the sample, not errors in the process.

stem: An experiment tested four fertilizers on plant growth. Two grams of each were diluted in 9 mL of water. Which measuring device would introduce the least error into the measurement of the 9 mL?
explanation: Use the graduated cylinder closest in size to what you are measuring. 9 mL is closest to 10 mL capacity. Smaller cylinders have finer graduation marks, making them MORE precise for measuring small volumes. A 100 mL cylinder measuring 9 mL would have huge potential for error.

stem: A student is trying to determine the effect of pollutants on the growth of three groups of seeds. The results will not be valid because the experiment has no —
explanation: Without a control group (seeds grown WITHOUT pollutants), there is no baseline for comparison. You cannot determine whether the pollutants caused any effect on growth. The experiment HAS variables (pollutants = IV, growth = DV) and can still have a hypothesis and conclusion — but without a control, the conclusion would not be valid.

stem: Which sentence best states the importance of using control groups?

options.a: Control groups provide a method by which statistical variability can be reduced.
options.b: Control groups allow comparison between subjects receiving a treatment and those receiving no treatment.
options.c: Control groups eliminate the need for statistical tests and simplify calculations.
options.d: Control groups eliminate the need for large sample sizes.

explanation: The whole purpose of a control group is COMPARISON. It lets you see what happens when NO treatment is applied, so you can compare that baseline to the experimental group. "Control = Compare." Controls do NOT reduce variability, eliminate statistics, or replace large sample sizes.

stem: A biologist tracked a bluegill fish population: 200 in 1990, rose to 350 by 1993, held steady at 350 through 1996, then declined to 180 by 1999. During which period did the population decline?
explanation: The population DECLINED from 350 to 180 during 1996–1999. From 1990–1993, it was INCREASING (200 → 350). From 1993–1996, it was STABLE (350 → 350). "Decline" means the number went DOWN — only 1996–1999 fits.

stem: A graph shows lynx and hare populations from 1845 to 1940. If a predator of the lynx enters the food chain, you might expect the number of —

options.a: lynx and hares to become equal

explanation: If a new predator kills more lynx, the lynx population decreases. Fewer lynx means fewer hare predators, so the hare population INCREASES. This is a classic predator-prey chain: remove a predator → its prey increases. The SOL often tests this logic with graph data.

stem: According to a graph, the addition of the enzyme amylase to a starch solution causes the reaction to —
explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts — they SPEED UP chemical reactions. When amylase is added, the rate of starch breakdown increases dramatically. On a graph, you would see the reaction line shoot up much faster after the enzyme is added. This is a core concept that connects BIO.1c (graph reading) with BIO.2c (enzyme function).

stem: Based on the data in the table below, what is the difference between the mean number of sparrows and the mean number of jays observed at Willow Point between May 12 and May 16?
explanation: This is a straightforward data table calculation: 84 − 46 = 38. The SOL includes basic math questions like this to test whether you can read and calculate from data tables. Always double-check your subtraction — the wrong answers are designed to catch common arithmetic mistakes.

stem: Richard was observing black swallowtail butterflies in the field. Which one of these is an observation he made about a black swallowtail butterfly?

options.a: The black swallowtail belongs to the class Insecta.
options.b: The black swallowtail laid its eggs on a parsley plant.
options.c: The black swallowtail likes to fly more than anything else.
options.d: The black swallowtail is closely related to the spicebush swallowtail.

explanation: An OBSERVATION is something you directly SEE happen. Richard could directly see the butterfly lay eggs on parsley (B). Choice A is a classification — that requires prior knowledge from a taxonomy system, not field observation. Choice C uses the word "likes," which is an inference about the butterfly's preference — you cannot observe what an animal "likes." Choice D is an inference about evolutionary relationships based on analysis, not direct observation. Only B describes something you could witness firsthand in the field.

stem: A biology class of 24 students decides to measure the height of each student and then calculate the average height for the class. Which of these is a possible source of error in this activity?

options.a: The number of males and females in the class
options.b: The difference in the ages of the students in the class
options.c: The total number of students in the class
options.d: The accuracy of making and recording measurements

explanation: The question asks about a source of ERROR — something that could make the data unreliable or inaccurate. Choice D identifies a real source of error: if students measure heights inaccurately (reading the ruler wrong, rounding incorrectly, recording the wrong number), the data will contain errors. Choices A and B describe natural variation in the sample — those are real differences, not errors. Choice C (total number of students) is a characteristic of the sample, not an error source. Measurement error is one of the most common threats to data quality in any experiment.

stem: A study exposed wheat plants to different intensities of ultraviolet light and recorded seed production. Which of these statements is best supported by these data?

options.a: Chloroplasts of wheat are able to filter out low-intensity ultraviolet light.
options.b: Wheat plants exposed to high-intensity ultraviolet light produce fewer seeds.
options.c: No exposure to ultraviolet light increases pollination in wheat plants.
options.d: High-intensity ultraviolet light may be used to control weed growth.

explanation: This question tests whether you can draw a conclusion that matches the actual data. The study measured UV light intensity vs. seed production in WHEAT. Choice B directly describes what the data shows: high-intensity UV → fewer seeds. Choice A introduces chloroplast filtering — the study did not measure chloroplast function. Choice C introduces pollination — the study measured seed production, not pollination specifically. Choice D jumps to weed growth — the study was about wheat, not weeds. Always pick the conclusion that stays within the boundaries of what was actually measured.

stem: Which statement BEST describes the difference between a scientific theory and a scientific law?

options.a: A theory is a guess; a law is a proven fact.
options.b: A theory becomes a law after enough evidence supports it.
options.c: A theory explains WHY something happens; a law describes WHAT happens.
options.d: A law is more important than a theory in science.

explanation: Choice C is correct. A scientific theory explains the MECHANISM — the "why" behind a natural phenomenon (e.g., the Theory of Evolution explains how populations change through natural selection). A scientific law describes the PATTERN — the "what" that happens consistently (e.g., the Law of Segregation describes that alleles separate during gamete formation). Choice A is wrong because a theory is NOT a guess — it is extensively tested. Choice B is wrong because theories do NOT become laws; they serve different purposes. Choice D is wrong because neither is "more important" — they simply answer different questions.

stem: A climate scientist uses a computer simulation to predict global temperatures over the next 50 years. Which BEST describes the purpose of this model?

options.a: To prove that climate change is occurring
options.b: To replace the need for collecting real temperature data
options.c: To predict future conditions based on current data and trends
options.d: To eliminate all uncertainty about future temperatures

explanation: Models are used to PREDICT — they take current data and project it into conditions we cannot directly observe (in this case, the future). Choice A is wrong because models do not "prove" anything — they provide evidence and projections. Choice B is wrong because models supplement data collection, they do not replace it. Choice D is wrong because models always contain uncertainty — they are simplifications. Choice C correctly identifies the predictive purpose of the computer model.

stem: A student creates a diagram of a food web for a forest ecosystem. Which is a limitation of this model?

options.a: The diagram shows which organisms eat which other organisms.
options.b: The diagram cannot show every species and interaction in the ecosystem.
options.c: The diagram uses arrows to show the direction of energy flow.
options.d: The diagram can be used to predict what happens if a species is removed.

explanation: A limitation is something the model CANNOT do or something it oversimplifies. Choices A, C, and D describe things the food web diagram CAN do — those are its strengths. Choice B identifies a genuine limitation: no food web diagram includes every single species and every single feeding relationship in an ecosystem. It is a simplified representation. Real ecosystems have thousands of species and millions of interactions — a diagram on paper can only show the major ones.

stem: Scientists in the 1800s believed that cells arose spontaneously from non-living matter. After Pasteur's experiment disproved spontaneous generation, the cell theory was updated to include "all cells come from pre-existing cells." What BEST explains why the model changed?

options.a: New experimental evidence contradicted the old explanation.
options.b: Scientists decided the old model was too simple.
options.c: The old model was never accepted by the scientific community.
options.d: Technology was not available to test the old model.

explanation: Scientific models change when NEW EVIDENCE contradicts or extends the existing explanation. Pasteur's swan-neck flask experiment provided direct experimental evidence that microorganisms came from the air, not from spontaneous generation. This new evidence forced a revision of the model. Choice B is vague and inaccurate — the issue was not simplicity but incorrect claims. Choice C is wrong — spontaneous generation WAS widely accepted before Pasteur. Choice D is wrong — the technology (microscopes, flasks) DID exist. The answer is A: new evidence drove the change.

stem: A biologist uses the equation N = N₀e^(rt) to estimate how a bacterial population will grow over the next 24 hours. What type of model is this?
explanation: The equation N = N₀e^(rt) is a mathematical model — it uses numbers, variables, and a formula to describe the relationship between population size, growth rate, and time. A physical model is a 3D object you can touch. A diagram is a 2D visual drawing. A computer simulation uses software to run complex scenarios. An equation that expresses a relationship using math is, by definition, a mathematical model. The answer is D.

stem: If a student needs to do research on heredity for a science project, which of these sources is the most reliable resource?
explanation: A professional (scientific) journal is peer-reviewed — expert scientists have checked the methods, data, and conclusions before publication. A weekly news magazine (A) summarizes research but may oversimplify. A newspaper tabloid (B) is the least reliable — tabloids prioritize sensational stories over accuracy. A national newspaper (D) is better than a tabloid but still not peer-reviewed. For any science research project, a professional journal is always the most reliable source. The answer is C.

stem: A student researching a new discovery about the activity of mitochondria could find the most current and reliable information in a —
explanation: The question asks for MOST CURRENT and RELIABLE. A scientific journal (A) wins on both counts: it is peer-reviewed (reliable) and publishes new research continuously (current). A textbook (D) is reliable but NOT current — textbooks take years to update and may not include the latest discoveries. A newspaper (B) and news magazine (C) are current but not peer-reviewed. When the SOL emphasizes "new discovery" or "most current," a scientific journal is always the best choice because it publishes cutting-edge research that has been peer-reviewed. The answer is A.

stem: Which of the following best explains why a student researching genetics should use the most recent textbooks available?

options.a: No technologies from more than ten years ago are still in use.
options.b: Research in Mendelian genetics began very recently.
options.c: New discoveries frequently add to older knowledge in genetics.
options.d: Older textbooks are more difficult to understand.

explanation: Genetics is a rapidly advancing field — the Human Genome Project, CRISPR gene editing, and epigenetics are all relatively recent developments that older textbooks would not cover. Choice C correctly identifies this: new discoveries are constantly adding to our knowledge of genetics, so recent textbooks include more complete and up-to-date information. Choice A is wrong — many older technologies (microscopes, gel electrophoresis) are still in use. Choice B is wrong — Mendelian genetics began in the 1860s, not recently. Choice D is wrong — difficulty is not the reason to prefer newer textbooks. The answer is C.

stem: In 1869, DNA was discovered within the nuclei of cells. By the 1940s, scientists knew that chromosomes were made of both DNA and protein but did not know which was the genetic material. In the 1950s, scientists demonstrated that DNA is the material responsible for heredity. In 1953, using information collected by other scientists, an American biologist and an English physicist built a three-dimensional model of DNA. These discoveries best illustrate the importance of —

options.c: recent improvements in the scientific method

explanation: The passage describes multiple scientists over many decades, each building on the work of others: discovery of DNA (1869) → identifying chromosomes (1940s) → proving DNA carries heredity (1950s) → Watson and Crick using "information collected by other scientists" to build the model (1953). The key phrase is "using information collected by OTHER scientists" — this shows collaboration, not independent work. Choice A (replication) is about repeating experiments, which is not the focus here. Choice C is wrong — the scientific method did not recently change. Choice D is the opposite of what the passage describes. The answer is B: collaborative efforts among scientists.

stem: Which of these would be measured to determine the density of the polar bear population in Canada?

options.a: The mass of the bears at the Arctic Circle
options.b: The total number of bears minus the juvenile bears
options.c: The number of bears per square kilometer
options.d: The total number of bears seen per day

explanation: Population DENSITY = number of organisms per unit area. Bears per square kilometer directly measures density. Mass, total numbers, or daily sightings don't give density.

stem: A scientist observes that a pond appears green. Which is a testable hypothesis for this observation?

options.a: The pond is too warm for fish.
options.b: Algae growth increases with higher nitrogen levels in the pond water.
options.c: The pond is beautiful in summer.

explanation: A testable hypothesis has an IV and DV that can be measured. "Algae growth increases with higher nitrogen" has a clear IV (nitrogen levels) and DV (algae growth). "Beautiful" and "eventually" can't be measured.

stem: A student tests how light color affects plant growth. She uses red, blue, and white light on identical plants with the same soil and water. The CONSTANTS in this experiment are —

options.d: Soil type, water amount, and plant type

explanation: Constants are factors kept THE SAME across all groups. Soil, water, and plant type are identical for every group. Light color is the IV (changed). Plant growth is the DV (measured).

stem: Which of these is a properly written hypothesis?

options.a: If the amount of fertilizer increases, then plant height will increase, because nutrients promote growth.
options.c: I think the plant will grow.
options.d: The plant grew 5 cm in one week.

explanation: A proper hypothesis uses "If... then... because..." format with a clear IV (fertilizer), DV (height), and reasoning. B is an opinion. C lacks specifics. D is an observation/data point, not a hypothesis.

stem: Which method would provide the most accurate information for students collecting data about local attitudes concerning alternative fuels?

options.a: Track prices at nearby gas stations
options.b: Take an opinion poll of drivers
options.d: Listen to speeches given by politicians

explanation: To measure ATTITUDES (opinions), you need to directly ASK people — an opinion poll. Gas prices measure cost, not attitudes. Newspapers and politicians give secondhand information, not direct local attitudes.

stem: A student tested whether music affects plant growth using only 2 plants. A teacher suggested the results would be more reliable if the student —

options.a: used a different type of music
options.c: increased the number of plants tested

explanation: Larger sample size = more reliable results. With only 2 plants, one unusual result could skew everything. More plants reduce the effect of random variation and make the data more trustworthy.

stem: Before beginning a lab that involves heating chemicals, a student should FIRST —

options.b: put on safety goggles and review safety procedures

imageNote: Image from SOL review materials
explanation: Safety comes FIRST in any lab. Before handling chemicals or heat, students must wear proper protective equipment (goggles, lab coat) and review safety procedures. Conclusions and cleanup come AFTER the experiment.

stem: According to the graph above, how many mice will be born in week 5 if the trend continues?
imageNote: Image from SOL review materials

title.text: Number of Mice Born by Week

explanation: To predict a trend, extend the pattern. If births are increasing by a consistent amount each week, continue that pattern to week 5. Exponential growth in mouse populations leads to 160 by week 5.

stem: A line graph shows a population increasing rapidly, then leveling off. The point where the line levels off represents —

options.b: the carrying capacity of the environment

imageNote: Rendered natively (chart or table); imageUrl removed.
explanation: When a population graph levels off (plateaus), it has reached the carrying capacity — the maximum number of organisms the environment can support with available resources.

stem: This graph shows the activity rates of two enzymes (pepsin and trypsin) at different pH levels. This graph shows that —

options.a: More enzymes are present at a higher pH
options.b: pH affects the activity rate of enzymes
options.c: Pepsin is less effective at low pH than trypsin
options.d: Pepsin is less sensitive to pH than trypsin

imageNote: Image from SOL review materials
explanation: The graph shows enzyme activity CHANGES with pH — each enzyme has a different optimum pH. This directly supports that pH affects enzyme activity rates. The graph does not show enzyme amounts or compare sensitivities.

stem: This graph of sheep population in Tasmania from 1840 to 1920 suggests the carrying capacity was approximately —
imageNote: Image from SOL review materials
explanation: Carrying capacity is the maximum population an environment can sustain — shown on a graph where the line LEVELS OFF (plateaus). The sheep population leveled off at approximately 1.75 million.

stem: The data show the growth of two bean plants over several weeks using four different fertilizers. The experimental data would be more valid if which of the following variables was included?

options.b: The plants were grown at variable temperatures.
options.d: A control without fertilizer was included for each plant.

explanation: Without a control group (plants with NO fertilizer), there is no baseline for comparison. You cannot determine if the fertilizers caused any growth difference. More fertilizer types or variable temperatures would not improve validity — a control would.

stem: A study finds that students who eat breakfast score higher on tests. A student concludes that eating breakfast CAUSES higher test scores. This conclusion is —

options.a: supported because the data show a clear trend
options.c: not supported because correlation does not prove causation
options.d: not supported because the sample size was too small

explanation: The data show a CORRELATION (two things happen together) but not CAUSATION (one directly causes the other). Maybe students who eat breakfast also sleep more, study harder, etc. Without a controlled experiment, you can't prove causation.

stem: A scientific theory is different from a hypothesis because a theory —

options.b: is a well-tested explanation supported by a large body of evidence
options.c: has been proven to be an absolute fact

explanation: A hypothesis is a testable prediction (starting point). A theory is extensively tested and supported by evidence from many experiments (explains WHY). Theories don't become laws — they serve different purposes. Theories are never "proven facts" but are the strongest explanations we have.

stem: In 1869, DNA was discovered within the nuclei of cells. By the 1940s, scientists knew chromosomes were made of DNA and protein. In the 1950s, scientists demonstrated DNA is the hereditary material. In 1953, using information from other scientists, an American biologist and an English physicist built a model of DNA. These discoveries best illustrate the importance of —

options.c: Recent improvements in the scientific method

explanation: Each discovery BUILT on previous scientists' work — from DNA discovery (1869) to the double helix model (1953). Multiple scientists across decades worked together. This is collaborative effort, not independent work.

stem: Which of the following scientific achievements best represents a collaborative effort among scientists?

options.a: Gregor Mendel's study of pea plants enabled Thomas Morgan to become the first to locate a gene on a chromosome in Drosophila.
options.b: Anton van Leeuwenhoek invented the microscope in the 1600s, which was later used by others to study cells.
options.c: Robert Koch studied infectious diseases, and Louis Pasteur demonstrated that life only comes from life.
options.d: Francis Crick and James Watson worked together to design the double-helix model of DNA.

explanation: Collaboration means scientists working TOGETHER on the same project. Crick and Watson directly worked together to build the DNA model — that is true collaboration. The other choices describe scientists working separately on related but independent projects.

stem: Early classification systems consisted of two kingdoms: Plantae and Animalia. What scientific development allowed taxonomists to establish the Monera and Protista kingdoms?
explanation: Monera (bacteria) and Protista (single-celled eukaryotes) are microscopic organisms. The electron microscope allowed scientists to see the detailed structures of these tiny organisms, revealing they were fundamentally different from plants and animals. New technology revised the scientific model of classification.

stem: The unique properties of water enable life to exist on Earth. Which of these is a property of pure water?

options.b: Its solid form is more dense than its liquid.
options.c: It is slightly more acidic than air.
options.d: It has a low heat absorption capacity.

explanation: Water is the "universal solvent" because its polarity allows it to dissolve many ionic and polar substances. B is backwards (ice is LESS dense). C compares water to air (nonsense). D is backwards (water has HIGH specific heat).

stem: Ice floats on a lake. This characteristic of water is responsible for —

options.c: preventing a lake from freezing solid

explanation: Ice is less dense than liquid water, so it floats on top and forms an insulating layer. This prevents the entire lake from freezing solid, allowing aquatic organisms to survive beneath the ice during winter.

stem: Some peeled pieces of apple were placed in distilled water and some in very salty water. The cells in the apple pieces will —

options.b: gain water in the distilled water and lose water in the salty water
options.d: lose water in the distilled water and gain water in the salty water

explanation: In distilled water (hypotonic), water moves INTO the apple cells by osmosis (cells swell). In salty water (hypertonic), water moves OUT of the cells (cells shrink/wilt). Water always moves toward the side with more solute.

stem: Unlike other animals, mammals can perspire. The main benefit of perspiring is that it —

options.a: removes dirt from the surface of the skin
options.d: removes extra water from the cells

explanation: Perspiration (sweating) cools the body through evaporative cooling. When sweat evaporates from the skin, the highest-energy (hottest) molecules leave first, taking heat with them and lowering the skin's temperature. This is a direct application of water's evaporative cooling property.

stem: Proteins are formed from monomers (subunits) called —
explanation: Proteins are polymers of amino acids. Fatty acids are monomers of lipids. Nucleotides are monomers of nucleic acids. Nucleic acids are the macromolecule itself, not a monomer.

stem: Both lipids and carbohydrates are important in animal cells because both —
explanation: Both lipids AND carbohydrates store energy (lipids for long-term, carbohydrates for short-term). Insulation is only lipids, not carbohydrates. Cell walls are made of cellulose (a carbohydrate) in plants, but animal cells don't have cell walls. Nitrogen is found in proteins and nucleic acids, not typically in lipids or carbohydrates.

stem: Amino acids link together by peptide bonds to form proteins. In which cellular organelle would this process occur?
explanation: Ribosomes are the organelles that assemble amino acids into proteins (translation). Mitochondria produce ATP. Lysosomes break down waste. The Golgi body packages and ships proteins after they're made.

stem: Which of the following molecules is most abundant in the cells of the human body?
explanation: Water makes up about 60-70% of the human body by mass. While all four answer choices are found in cells, water is by far the most abundant molecule. This connects BIO.2a (water chemistry) with BIO.2b (macromolecules) — water is the medium in which all macromolecules function.

stem: What is the function of enzymes in biological systems?

options.a: Enzymes act as products to create new chemical reactions.
options.b: Enzymes act as substrates when the necessary proteins are unavailable.
options.c: Enzymes bond with substrates to create the new reaction products.
options.d: Enzymes act as catalysts to drive chemical reactions forward.

explanation: Enzymes are biological catalysts — they speed up chemical reactions. They are NOT products, they are NOT substrates, and while they do temporarily bind substrates, they don't permanently "bond with" them to create products. They lower activation energy and are released unchanged.

stem: According to a graph, addition of the enzyme amylase causes the reaction to —
explanation: Enzymes are catalysts — they SPEED UP chemical reactions by lowering activation energy. Amylase specifically catalyzes the breakdown of starch into simple sugars. On a graph, you would see the reaction rate increase dramatically after the enzyme is added.

stem: Peroxidase is an enzyme that breaks down hydrogen peroxide in cells. It accomplishes this because of its structure. What part of the enzyme is involved in catalytic activity?
explanation: The active site is the specific region of an enzyme where the substrate binds and the catalytic reaction occurs. Quaternary structure refers to how multiple protein chains assemble. Pleated sheets are a type of protein secondary structure. "Binding pocket" sounds close but is not the standard biology term.

stem: Most cellular activities are processes regulated by the action of —
explanation: Enzymes regulate nearly all cellular activities. They control the rate of metabolic reactions including digestion, energy production, DNA replication, and protein synthesis. Polysaccharides store energy, lipids provide membranes and energy storage, and carbohydrates provide quick energy — but none of these REGULATE cellular processes the way enzymes do.

stem: A graph showing enzyme activity at different pH levels demonstrates that —

options.a: more enzymes are present at a higher pH
options.b: pepsin is less sensitive to pH than trypsin
options.c: pepsin is less effective at low pH than trypsin
options.d: pH affects the activity rate of enzymes

explanation: The graph shows that enzyme activity changes with pH — each enzyme has an optimal pH and loses activity at other pH values. This demonstrates that pH affects enzyme activity rate. Pepsin actually works BEST at low pH (stomach acid), so C is backwards.

stem: During transcription, an mRNA strand is built from a DNA template. If the DNA template reads TAC, what is the mRNA codon?
explanation: Apply the base-pairing rules: T→A, A→U, C→G. So DNA: TAC becomes mRNA: AUG. Choice B (ATG) uses DNA bases, not RNA — remember, RNA uses U instead of T. AUG is also the start codon (codes for methionine).

stem: Where does transcription occur in a eukaryotic cell?
explanation: Transcription (DNA → mRNA) occurs in the NUCLEUS, where the DNA is located. Translation (mRNA → protein) occurs at the RIBOSOME in the cytoplasm. Many students confuse these two locations.

stem: What is the role of tRNA during translation?

options.a: It copies the DNA sequence into mRNA
options.b: It carries specific amino acids to the ribosome
options.c: It holds the two strands of DNA together
options.d: It breaks down proteins into amino acids

explanation: Transfer RNA (tRNA) carries amino acids to the ribosome during translation. Each tRNA has an anticodon that matches a specific mRNA codon, ensuring the correct amino acid is delivered. Choice A describes RNA polymerase's job during transcription.

stem: Which of the following correctly describes the Central Dogma of molecular biology?
explanation: The Central Dogma flows in one direction: DNA is transcribed into RNA, and RNA is translated into Protein. DNA → RNA → Protein. Information flows from the gene (DNA) to the messenger (RNA) to the functional product (protein).

stem: The energy in food must be changed into a form that cells can use. The energy-transferring molecule used by cells is —
explanation: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the energy currency of the cell. Cells break down food (glucose) through cellular respiration and capture the released energy in ATP molecules. CO₂ is a waste product of respiration. RNA carries genetic instructions. DNA stores genetic information.

stem: Which of these processes is carried out in the same way in both plants and animals?
explanation: Both plants and animals break down glucose through cellular respiration in their mitochondria to produce ATP. The process is essentially the same in both. Excretion, circulation, and reproduction work very differently in plants vs. animals.

stem: Unlike plants, fungi cannot make their own food because they do not have —
explanation: Photosynthesis requires chlorophyll to capture light energy. Fungi lack chlorophyll, so they cannot photosynthesize and must obtain food by decomposing organic matter (they're heterotrophs/decomposers). Spores are for reproduction, roots are for absorption in plants, and hyphae are the thread-like structures fungi DO have.

stem: Which of the following is most effective in helping rain forest plants trap sunlight so that light energy can be converted to chemical energy?
explanation: Leaves contain chloroplasts with chlorophyll that capture sunlight for photosynthesis. Larger leaves = more surface area = more sunlight captured. Rain forest plants compete for limited light on the forest floor, so large leaves are an adaptation to maximize light capture. Roots absorb water, not light.

stem: In the human body, muscle cells have an increased need for energy during exercise. To help supply this energy, the body will immediately increase —

options.a: the breathing rate to supply more oxygen to cells for the release of energy
options.b: activity in the nervous system to stimulate intake of carbon dioxide
options.c: the need for waste products to be retained
options.d: food intake to increase substances available for respiration

explanation: During exercise, muscles need more ATP. Cellular respiration requires oxygen to efficiently break down glucose into ATP. Your body's immediate response is to increase breathing rate to get more oxygen to the muscles. You don't immediately eat more food (that takes hours to process), and the body eliminates CO₂ waste rather than retaining it.

stem: If transpiration stopped completely, how would a plant's homeostasis first be affected?

options.a: Water molecules would not be released from leaves
options.b: Carbohydrates would no longer be formed
options.c: Fewer sugars would diffuse into the soil
options.d: More CO₂ molecules would be taken in by leaves

explanation: Transpiration is the evaporation of water from plant leaves through stomata. If it stopped, the most immediate effect is that water is no longer released from leaves, disrupting the plant's water balance (homeostasis).

stem: According to a simplified water cycle diagram, the process of transpiration is the process that —

options.b: increases the rate of the water cycle

explanation: Transpiration is the loss of water vapor from plant leaves through stomata. It cools the plant (evaporative cooling) and helps pull water up through the xylem.

stem: When there is a lower concentration of water outside of a plant cell rather than inside, the plant will tend to —
explanation: Lower water concentration outside = hypertonic solution = water leaves the cell by osmosis. The plant loses turgor pressure and wilts.

stem: The concentration of glucose must be maintained within a fairly narrow range in most vertebrates. This statement is an example of —
explanation: Maintaining glucose within a narrow range is homeostasis — the body's ability to maintain stable internal conditions. Insulin and glucagon regulate blood glucose levels.

stem: Which macromolecules function in insulation, helping animals conserve heat?
explanation: Lipids (fats) form insulating layers beneath the skin of animals, helping conserve body heat.

stem: Proteins are formed from monomers called —
explanation: Amino acids are the monomers of proteins. There are 20 different amino acids linked by peptide bonds.

stem: Amino acids link together by peptide bonds to form proteins. In which organelle does this occur?
explanation: Translation (protein assembly) occurs at the ribosome.

stem: Enzymes only work with specific substrates because each substrate —

options.c: can only use a specific ionic bond with the enzyme
options.d: has a specific activation site for enzyme attachment

explanation: Each substrate has a shape that specifically fits into the enzyme's active site (lock-and-key model).

stem: A DNA template strand reads TAC. During transcription, the mRNA codon produced would be —
explanation: Apply base-pairing rules for transcription: T→A, A→U, C→G. DNA: TAC becomes mRNA: AUG. Remember RNA uses U instead of T, so choice B (ATG) is wrong — that's DNA, not RNA. AUG is also the start codon (codes for methionine).

stem: The process of photosynthesis ultimately converts light energy into —
explanation: Photosynthesis converts light (radiant) energy from the sun into chemical energy stored in the bonds of glucose (sugar). That chemical energy is later released through cellular respiration.

stem: Photosynthesis is important for almost all life on Earth because it —
explanation: Photosynthesis produces the oxygen that most organisms need for cellular respiration. It also produces glucose (food).

stem: Algae and multicellular plants are autotrophs because they —
explanation: Autotrophs make their own food. Plants and algae do this through photosynthesis — capturing sunlight to produce glucose.

stem: Photosynthesis and respiration can be thought of as a cycle because —

options.a: one is used only by plants and the other only by animals
options.b: both give off oxygen to be used by animals
options.c: the products of one are used as the raw materials of the other
options.d: they both have the same purpose

explanation: Photosynthesis produces glucose + O₂ which are consumed by respiration. Respiration produces CO₂ + H₂O which are consumed by photosynthesis.

stem: The Central Dogma of molecular biology describes the flow of genetic information as —
explanation: The Central Dogma: DNA is transcribed into RNA (in the nucleus), and RNA is translated into Protein (at the ribosome). Information flows in one direction: DNA → RNA → Protein.

stem: A diagram models a cellular metabolic process. The main purpose of this process is to produce —
explanation: The main purpose of cellular respiration is to produce usable energy in the form of ATP. ADP and H₂O are byproducts.

stem: A mushroom and a humpback whale are alike because both are —
explanation: Both mushrooms (fungi) and whales (animals) are heterotrophs — they cannot make their own food and must consume other organisms. Mushrooms are not motile, neither is prokaryotic, and mushrooms are multicellular.

stem: A microorganism releases water to regulate its salinity during osmosis, similar to a human sweating on a hot day. This process that keeps body fluids in balance is known as —
explanation: Homeostasis is the maintenance of stable internal conditions. Both the microorganism regulating salinity and the human sweating are examples of homeostasis.

stem: What characteristic do all living things share?

options.b: They are made up of many parts.

explanation: All living things contain DNA (expanded cell theory: DNA is passed cell-to-cell). Not all organisms reproduce by mitosis (some use binary fission). Not all are "many parts" (unicellular organisms). Not all need oxygen (some bacteria are anaerobic).

stem: What structure is common to ALL five kingdoms of living organisms?
explanation: ALL cells contain DNA (expanded cell theory). Not all have cell walls (animal cells don't). Not all have a nucleus (prokaryotes don't). Not all have mitochondria (prokaryotes don't).

stem: A data table comparing cell features across kingdoms shows that eukaryotic cells vary in covering and food production. This supports which part of cell theory?

options.a: All cells have the same basic composition but differ in structure
options.b: Cells can only come from other cells
options.d: Cells are too small to see without a microscope

explanation: The data showing variation in cell covering (wall vs. no wall) and food production (autotroph vs. heterotroph) supports the expanded cell theory: all cells share the same basic chemical composition, but they can have different structures for different functions.

stem: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between scientific theories and laws?

options.a: Theories become laws once they are proven
options.b: Laws are more valid than theories
options.d: Theories explain why; laws describe what happens — both are equally valid

explanation: Theories and laws serve different purposes but are EQUALLY valid in science. Theories explain WHY phenomena occur. Laws describe WHAT happens consistently. A theory never "becomes" a law — they are different types of scientific knowledge.

stem: Which of these is responsible for the "rough" appearance of endoplasmic reticulum?
explanation: The rough ER is "rough" because it is studded with ribosomes on its surface. These ribosomes are making proteins that will be transported through the ER to the Golgi for packaging. Smooth ER lacks ribosomes and makes lipids instead.

stem: Compared to a skin cell, a muscle cell is likely to have more —
explanation: Muscle cells contract constantly and need enormous amounts of energy (ATP). Mitochondria produce ATP through cellular respiration. Therefore, muscle cells have MORE mitochondria than skin cells. This is a classic "form fits function" question. Human cells never have chloroplasts.

stem: Which of the following organelles is present in BOTH prokaryotes and eukaryotes?
explanation: Ribosomes are found in ALL cells because ALL cells need to make proteins. ER, Golgi, and nucleus are membrane-bound organelles found only in eukaryotes. Prokaryotes lack all membrane-bound organelles.

stem: What repackages proteins into forms the cell can use, expel, or keep stored?
explanation: The Golgi apparatus (Golgi bodies) receives proteins from the ER, modifies and packages them, then ships them to their destination. Think of it as the "shipping department" of the cell. Lysosomes break things down. Mitochondria make energy. Centrioles help with cell division.

stem: Which pair of structures best shows that plant cells have functions different from animal cells?
explanation: Chloroplasts (for photosynthesis) and cell walls (for rigid support) are found in plant cells but NOT animal cells. Cytoplasm, mitochondria, ribosomes, cell membranes, and nuclei are found in BOTH plant and animal cells.

stem: Which of the following phases is the first step in mitosis?
explanation: PMAT: Prophase is the FIRST stage of mitosis. During prophase, chromosomes condense and become visible, and the nuclear membrane begins to break down.

stem: The process of DNA replication is necessary before a cell —
explanation: DNA must be copied (replicated) before a cell divides so that each daughter cell receives a complete set of genetic instructions. Transcription and translation happen throughout the cell's life, not specifically before division.

stem: The jimsonweed normally has 12 chromosomes in body cells. How many chromosomes will an egg cell have?
explanation: Egg cells (gametes) are produced by meiosis, which cuts the chromosome number in HALF. If body cells have 12 chromosomes, egg cells have 12 ÷ 2 = 6. This ensures that when egg + sperm combine at fertilization, the offspring has the correct number (6 + 6 = 12).

stem: The processes of meiosis and fertilization help ensure the survival of the species by providing each generation with the same number of —
explanation: Meiosis halves the chromosome number in gametes. Fertilization combines two gametes, restoring the full chromosome number. This cycle ensures each generation has the SAME number of chromosomes as the parents.

stem: Unicellular organisms cannot grow very large because the —

options.a: diffusion of nutrients into the cell's interior would be too slow
options.b: locomotion of the organisms would be too slow
options.c: respiratory rate would be too high
options.d: energy expenditures would be too great

explanation: As cells grow larger, the surface-area-to-volume ratio decreases. The cell membrane can't transport nutrients fast enough via diffusion to reach the interior of a very large cell. This is why multicellular organisms use many small cells instead of one giant cell.

stem: In an osmosis diagram, only water molecules could enter the cell because water molecules —

options.a: have more energy than the protein molecules
options.b: are smaller than the protein molecules
options.c: contain more hydrogen atoms than the protein molecules
options.d: are more numerous than the protein molecules

explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable. Water molecules are small enough to pass through, while large protein molecules are too big. Size determines what can cross the membrane during osmosis.

stem: A bacterium can recognize different substances in its environment. What part of the bacterium allows this?
explanation: The cell membrane contains receptor proteins that detect chemicals in the environment. Bacteria don't have a true nucleus (they're prokaryotes), and they lack membrane-bound organelles like lysosomes and ER.

stem: Which type of transport requires the cell to use ATP energy?
explanation: Active transport is the ONLY type that requires ATP energy because it moves substances AGAINST the concentration gradient (from low to high concentration). Diffusion, osmosis, and facilitated diffusion are all passive — no energy needed.

stem: Which of these organisms contains no specialized cells?
explanation: Paramecium is a unicellular organism — it has only one cell, so there is no cell specialization. Sea anemones, jellyfish, and sponges are all multicellular animals with at least some specialized cell types.

stem: A cell with numerous ribosomes is probably specialized for —
explanation: Ribosomes make proteins. A cell with many ribosomes is specialized to produce large amounts of protein (like an immune cell making antibodies or a pancreas cell making insulin). Many mitochondria would indicate energy production.

stem: Some unicellular organisms are motile (have the ability to move). Which cellular structures are associated with movement?
explanation: Flagella (and cilia) are cellular structures used for movement. A flagellum is a whip-like tail that propels cells through liquid. Chloroplasts are for photosynthesis, ribosomes for protein synthesis, and vacuoles for storage.

stem: When a Paramecium takes in food, the food is contained in which organelle?
explanation: In unicellular organisms like Paramecium, food is taken in through endocytosis and stored/digested in a food vacuole. This is analogous to the stomach in multicellular animals. Chloroplasts make food (plants only), mitochondria make ATP, and the nucleus contains DNA.

stem: The main difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells is that —

options.a: eukaryotic cells have a smaller cell nucleus
options.b: prokaryotic cells are always much larger
options.c: prokaryotic cells do not have a plasma membrane
options.d: eukaryotic cells have a more advanced cellular organization

explanation: Eukaryotic cells have membrane-bound organelles including a true nucleus, giving them more complex organization. Prokaryotes are simpler — no membrane-bound nucleus or organelles. Both have cell membranes, so C is wrong.

stem: Which characteristic makes prokaryotic organisms different from eukaryotes?

options.d: Prokaryotic cells do not have membrane-bound organelles

explanation: Prokaryotes lack membrane-bound organelles (including a true nucleus). They ARE made of cells, they DO have DNA, and they DO have chromosomes (just not enclosed in a nucleus).

stem: Which is responsible for the "rough" appearance of endoplasmic reticulum?
explanation: Ribosomes studded on the ER surface make it appear "rough." These ribosomes are making proteins for transport.

stem: Which of these functions most like the "brain" of a cell?
explanation: The nucleus contains the cell's DNA and controls all cell activities, making it the "brain" or control center. The mitochondrion produces energy. The Golgi packages proteins. The smooth ER makes lipids.

stem: In the human body, the circulatory system transports substances. Which organelle performs a similar function within the cell?
explanation: The ER is the cell's internal transport network, moving proteins and lipids throughout the cell, similar to the circulatory system in the body.

stem: Which organelle is present in BOTH prokaryotes and eukaryotes?
explanation: Ribosomes are found in ALL cells because ALL cells need to make proteins.

stem: Which pair of structures best shows that plant cells have different functions from animal cells?
explanation: Chloroplasts and cell walls are unique to plant cells. The other options exist in both.

stem: Which phase is the first step in mitosis?
explanation: PMAT: Prophase is first. Chromosomes condense and nuclear membrane breaks down.

stem: DNA replication is necessary before a cell —
explanation: DNA must be copied before division so each daughter cell gets a complete set of instructions.

stem: How is a prokaryotic bacterium different from a eukaryotic cell? It —

options.a: stores its genetic information in DNA

explanation: Prokaryotic cells lack a membrane-bound nucleus — their DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm. Both prokaryotes and eukaryotes have DNA, ribosomes, and cell membranes, so A, B, and D are shared features, not differences.

stem: In a cell membrane model, the molecules that move large molecules in and out of the cell are —
explanation: Transport proteins in the membrane help move large molecules across. Lipids form the bilayer structure.

stem: Unicellular organisms cannot grow very large because —

options.a: diffusion of nutrients would be too slow
options.c: respiratory rate would be too high
options.d: energy expenditures would be too great

explanation: As cells grow, surface-area-to-volume ratio decreases. The membrane can't transport nutrients fast enough via diffusion to support a very large cell.

stem: What part of a bacterium allows it to recognize substances in its environment?
explanation: The cell membrane has receptor proteins that detect chemicals. Bacteria lack nucleus, lysosomes, and ER.

stem: Which organism contains no specialized cells?
explanation: Paramecium is unicellular — one cell, no specialization. The others are multicellular.

stem: Compared to a skin cell, a muscle cell likely has more —
explanation: Muscle cells need lots of ATP for contraction = lots of mitochondria. Human cells never have chloroplasts.

stem: What is the purpose of the flagellum?
explanation: A flagellum is a whip-like tail used for cell movement/locomotion.

stem: Which characteristic do viruses possess in common with living cells?

options.a: They contain nucleic acids such as DNA or RNA.
options.b: They contain a nucleus and organelles.
options.d: They are inactive outside the body of living cells.

explanation: Both viruses and living cells contain nucleic acids (DNA or RNA). Viruses do NOT have a nucleus, organelles, or the ability to make food. Being "inactive outside cells" is true of viruses but NOT something shared with living cells.

stem: An important difference between viruses and living cells is that viruses —

options.d: need an energy source but cells do not

explanation: Viruses CANNOT reproduce on their own — they must infect a living host cell and hijack its machinery. This is the key reason viruses are considered nonliving. Viruses CAN mutate (that's why flu shots change every year), they don't have nuclei, and all cells DO need energy.

stem: In the lysogenic cycle, what happens to the viral DNA after it enters the host cell?

options.a: It immediately makes copies of the virus
options.b: It is destroyed by the host cell's enzymes
options.c: It integrates into the host's DNA and is copied with each cell division
options.d: It exits the cell and infects neighboring cells

explanation: In the lysogenic cycle, viral DNA inserts itself into the host cell's chromosome and becomes a provirus. It is copied every time the host divides, lying dormant until a trigger activates the lytic cycle. Choice A describes the lytic cycle, not lysogenic.

stem: What is the final step of the lytic cycle?

options.a: Viral DNA integrates into host chromosome
options.b: The host cell bursts, releasing new viruses
options.c: The virus attaches to the host cell surface
options.d: The host cell begins normal cell division

explanation: The lytic cycle ends with LYSIS — the host cell bursts open, releasing hundreds of new virus particles that go on to infect other cells. "Lytic" comes from "lysis" meaning to break open. Integration into host DNA is the lysogenic cycle.

stem: Which of the following is TRUE about bacteria but NOT true about viruses?
explanation: Bacteria can reproduce on their own through binary fission. Viruses CANNOT reproduce independently — they must use a host cell. Both bacteria and viruses contain genetic material, can cause disease, and can mutate.

stem: Overuse of antibiotics has led to antibiotic-resistant strains of bacteria in a relatively short time because —

options.a: there are many different types of bacteria
options.c: travelers carry bacteria around the world

explanation: Bacteria can divide every 20 minutes. When antibiotics kill most bacteria but a few resistant ones survive, those resistant bacteria reproduce rapidly and become the dominant population. Fast reproduction = fast evolution of resistance.

stem: Deep-sea bacteria near hydrothermal vents use chemical energy from inorganic compounds to make food. These bacteria are classified as —
explanation: Chemoautotrophs use chemical energy (not sunlight) to make their own food from inorganic compounds. Photoautotrophs use sunlight. Heterotrophs consume other organisms. Deep-sea vents have no sunlight, so the bacteria must use chemical energy.

stem: The increasing resistance of M. tuberculosis to streptomycin is most likely due to the increased —

options.a: use of streptomycin for bacterial infections

explanation: Overuse of an antibiotic kills susceptible bacteria but leaves resistant ones alive to reproduce. The more streptomycin is used, the more opportunity for resistant M. tuberculosis to survive and spread. This is natural selection in action.

stem: The results of Pasteur's experiment helped Pasteur to —

options.a: isolate the virus responsible for smallpox
options.c: reject the theory of spontaneous generation

explanation: Pasteur's famous swan-neck flask experiment proved that microorganisms come from other microorganisms, not from non-living matter. This disproved the widely-held theory of spontaneous generation and was a critical step in developing the germ theory of disease.

stem: Animals coming from foreign countries are kept in quarantine before being allowed into the United States. This precaution is taken to help prevent —

options.a: the escape of foreign animals into local natural habitats
options.b: the introduction of foreign animal diseases
options.c: cross-breeding of native animals with the foreign ones
options.d: predator destruction of the foreign animals

explanation: Quarantine isolates potentially infected organisms to prevent disease from spreading to the local population. This is a direct application of germ theory — if disease is caused by microorganisms, keeping infected animals separate prevents transmission.

stem: A certain virus causes a disease of the digestive system. What is the most likely source of this virus?
explanation: Digestive system diseases are typically transmitted through contaminated water or food (fecal-oral route). Respiratory viruses spread through air. Soil-borne diseases involve wounds. Insect-borne diseases include malaria and West Nile (spread by mosquitoes).

stem: People moved from cities during smallpox epidemics but still contracted the disease from family members. Smallpox transmission was discovered to be by —

options.a: being bitten by mosquitoes that carry the virus
options.b: directly contacting a person with smallpox
options.d: eating food that contains the virus

explanation: The passage describes people who moved away from cities but brought infected family members with them — and STILL got sick. This showed that smallpox spreads through direct contact with infected individuals, not through air, water, or food.

stem: Which is NOT a method of preventing bacterial illness from spreading in a meat-processing plant?

options.a: Using antibiotics to treat persons with food poisoning
options.b: Washing hands and utensils with hot soapy water
options.c: Use of gloves when handling raw meat products
options.d: Treatment of work areas with chemical disinfectants

explanation: Antibiotics TREAT infections after they happen; they don't PREVENT bacteria from spreading in a facility. Prevention requires hygiene practices: handwashing, gloves, and disinfection.

stem: Which of these could be successfully treated with antibiotics?
explanation: Strep throat is caused by a BACTERIUM (Streptococcus), so it can be treated with antibiotics. The common cold, HIV, and influenza are caused by VIRUSES — antibiotics have no effect on them.

stem: How do vaccines prevent disease?

options.a: They kill all viruses in the body
options.b: They provide antibiotics that fight infection
options.c: They expose the immune system to a weakened pathogen so it builds immunity
options.d: They strengthen the cell wall to block viruses

explanation: Vaccines contain dead or weakened pathogens that trigger an immune response WITHOUT causing disease. The immune system creates antibodies and memory cells that provide protection against future exposure to the real pathogen.

stem: Overuse of antibiotics has led to resistant bacteria in a short time because —

options.a: there are many different types of bacteria
options.c: travelers carry bacteria around the world

explanation: Bacteria can divide every 20 minutes. Rapid reproduction means resistant individuals quickly become the dominant population. This is natural selection: the antibiotic selects FOR resistant bacteria by killing the non-resistant ones.

stem: A virus consists of —

options.a: nucleic acid surrounded by a protein coat (capsid)
options.b: a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and ribosomes
options.c: a cell wall, circular DNA, and flagellum

explanation: A virus = nucleic acid core (DNA or RNA) + capsid (protein coat). That's it. Viruses lack all cellular structures like membranes, ribosomes, and organelles. Choices B, C, and D describe parts of actual cells.

stem: Why are viruses considered nonliving?

options.a: They cannot carry out life processes without a host cell
options.b: They are too small to be seen
options.c: They do not contain any genetic material

explanation: Viruses cannot reproduce, metabolize, or carry out ANY life process without hijacking a host cell. They DO contain genetic material (DNA or RNA), so C is wrong. Viruses infect all types of organisms, not just plants.

stem: A virus that integrates its DNA into a host cell's chromosome and remains dormant is in the —
explanation: In the lysogenic cycle, viral DNA integrates into the host chromosome as a provirus and lies dormant, being copied with each cell division until a trigger activates the lytic cycle.

stem: The lytic cycle ends when —

options.a: viral DNA integrates into host chromosome
options.b: the host cell begins normal division
options.c: the virus exits without damaging the host
options.d: the host cell bursts, releasing new viruses

explanation: Lytic = lysis = burst. The host cell is destroyed, releasing hundreds of new virus particles that infect neighboring cells. Integration into the chromosome is the lysogenic cycle.

stem: Bacteria transfer genetic material between cells through a pilus during a process called —
explanation: Conjugation is the rare sexual reproduction in bacteria where DNA (often a plasmid) is transferred through a pilus connecting two cells. Binary fission is asexual splitting. Transduction is virus-mediated gene transfer.

stem: Which is TRUE about bacteria but NOT viruses?
explanation: Bacteria reproduce independently through binary fission. Viruses CANNOT reproduce without a host cell. Both contain genetic material, cause disease, and can mutate.

stem: Antibiotic-resistant strains of bacteria have developed in a relatively short time because —

options.a: there are many different types of bacteria
options.c: travelers carry bacteria around the world

explanation: Bacteria can divide every 20 minutes. Rapid reproduction means resistant individuals quickly become the dominant population through natural selection.

stem: Cyanobacteria are classified as photoautotrophs because they —

options.c: use sunlight to make their own food

explanation: Photo = light, auto = self. Photoautotrophs use sunlight to make their own food, just like plants. Cyanobacteria were the first organisms to produce oxygen through photosynthesis.

stem: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria are essential for plant growth because they —

options.b: convert atmospheric nitrogen into forms plants can absorb
options.c: kill harmful fungi in the soil
options.d: break down plant cell walls for easier nutrient absorption

explanation: Plants need nitrogen to build proteins and DNA but cannot use N₂ gas from the atmosphere. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria (like Rhizobium in legume root nodules) convert N₂ into ammonia (NH₃), which plants CAN absorb.

stem: Bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria) contribute to ecosystems by —

options.a: killing bacteria and releasing nutrients back into the environment
options.d: preventing all bacterial diseases in animals

explanation: When bacteriophages lyse (burst) bacteria, the bacterial cell contents (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus) are released back into the environment. This "viral shunt" recycles enormous amounts of nutrients in ocean ecosystems.

stem: Smallpox transmission was discovered to be by —
explanation: Smallpox spreads through direct person-to-person contact. People who moved from cities but brought infected family members still got sick, proving direct contact was the transmission method.

stem: Viruses can drive microbial evolution because they —

options.c: can transfer genes between different bacteria through transduction
options.d: produce antibiotics that kill competing bacteria

explanation: Through transduction, viruses accidentally pick up host DNA and transfer it to the next bacterium they infect. This moves genes (including antibiotic resistance) between bacteria, driving microbial evolution and genetic diversity.

stem: Koch's postulates established that —

options.a: all diseases are caused by viruses

explanation: Koch developed a 4-step process (isolate, culture, infect, re-isolate) to prove that a specific microorganism causes a specific disease. This was foundational to modern microbiology.

stem: A doctor prescribes antibiotics for a patient with strep throat but NOT for a patient with the flu. This is because antibiotics —

options.a: kill bacteria but have no effect on viruses
options.b: kill viruses but have no effect on bacteria
options.c: work equally well on both bacteria and viruses
options.d: are only available for throat infections

explanation: Strep throat is caused by bacteria (Streptococcus), so antibiotics work. The flu is caused by a virus (influenza), so antibiotics are useless. Antibiotics target bacterial structures that viruses don't have.

stem: Bacteria play an important role in ecosystems by —

options.a: producing oxygen through photosynthesis in all environments
options.b: consuming all dead organisms before they decompose
options.c: cycling nutrients by decomposing dead organisms
options.d: preventing all diseases in plants and animals

explanation: Bacteria are key decomposers that break down dead organisms and return nutrients (nitrogen, carbon, phosphorus) to the soil and atmosphere, keeping nutrient cycles running.

stem: Bacteria can reproduce independently by binary fission. What does this tell you?

options.d: Bacteria are living organisms, viruses are not

explanation: Independent reproduction is a key characteristic of life. Bacteria can reproduce on their own = living. Viruses cannot = nonliving. Bacteria are actually LARGER than viruses, contain DNA, and CAN cause disease.

stem: The parts of DNA that provide the code for proteins are the —
explanation: The sequence of nitrogenous bases (A, T, G, C) along the DNA strand is the genetic code. Each three-base sequence (codon) specifies one amino acid. Phosphates and sugars form the backbone structure but don't carry the code.

stem: DNA fits inside the nucleus because it can —

options.a: denature from the effect of an enzyme
options.c: extend to form very long, thin molecules

explanation: DNA coils around histone proteins, compacting into chromosomes that fit inside the nucleus. If DNA were stretched out straight, it would be about 2 meters long — far too long to fit without coiling.

stem: A triplet code for RNA may be represented by all of the following EXCEPT —
explanation: RNA uses Uracil (U) instead of Thymine (T). CGT contains T, which is a DNA base, not an RNA base. All other options use only valid RNA bases (C, G, U, A).

stem: What is one potential benefit of the Human Genome Project?

options.c: Classifying man most accurately in the animal kingdom
options.d: Finding the genes responsible for many diseases

explanation: The Human Genome Project sequenced all human DNA, enabling scientists to identify genes linked to diseases like cancer, diabetes, and sickle-cell anemia. This has led to genetic testing and targeted treatments.

stem: Wild cats experience decreased genetic diversity as populations decline. Decreased genetic diversity leads to —
explanation: Less genetic diversity = less variation in immune defenses = entire population vulnerable to the same diseases. Genetically diverse populations have individuals with different immune capabilities, so some survive any given disease.

stem: For a mutation to pass on to offspring, it must occur in a —
explanation: Only mutations in sex cells (gametes) are passed to offspring. Mutations in somatic cells (muscle, bone, brain) affect only that individual and die with them. This is a frequently tested distinction.

stem: In a cow heterozygous for long hair (Ll), what percentage of gametes will carry the dominant allele?
explanation: Heterozygous Ll: meiosis separates the alleles. Half the gametes get L (dominant) and half get l (recessive). So 50% carry the dominant allele.

stem: Inbreeding in endangered species over a short time may lead to extinction because it may —
explanation: Inbreeding reduces genetic diversity because related individuals share many of the same alleles. Less diversity = less ability to adapt to diseases or environmental changes = higher extinction risk.

stem: Tall (T) is dominant to short (t) in pea plants. If two heterozygous tall plants are crossed, what percent will be short?
explanation: Tt x Tt gives 1 TT : 2 Tt : 1 tt. Only tt (1 out of 4 = 25%) shows the short phenotype. The 3:1 ratio (75% tall : 25% short) is the classic Mendelian result.

stem: Cross TTPP x ttpp. Which describes the offspring?
explanation: TTPP can only pass T and P. ttpp can only pass t and p. All offspring are TtPp. Since T (tall) and P (purple) are dominant, ALL offspring show both dominant phenotypes.

stem: Two heterozygous short-haired rabbits (Ff x Ff) are crossed. What is the chance of offspring with short fur?
explanation: Short fur (F) is dominant. Ff x Ff gives: 1 FF + 2 Ff + 1 ff = 3 out of 4 show short fur (FF, Ff, Ff all show dominant phenotype). Only ff (1 in 4) shows long fur.

stem: Black rabbits crossed with white rabbits produce all black F1 offspring. When F1 rabbits are crossed, some white appear in F2. What can we conclude?

options.a: The white parent carried a dominant allele
options.b: All black rabbits in F2 are homozygous
options.d: All F1 rabbits carried a recessive allele

explanation: All F1 are black but produce some white F2 offspring. This means F1 must be heterozygous (Bb) — they carry the recessive white allele (b) but show the dominant black phenotype. When two Bb cross: 1 BB : 2 Bb : 1 bb.

stem: A family tree shows that albinism appears in offspring of normally pigmented parents. Albinism is —

options.d: requires both parents to be albinos

explanation: If normally pigmented parents produce albino offspring, both parents must be carriers (heterozygous). The trait only appears when two recessive alleles are inherited (homozygous recessive). This is classic recessive inheritance.

stem: Pink snapdragons crossed produce 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white offspring. This illustrates —
explanation: The 1:2:1 phenotype ratio with a BLENDED heterozygote (pink = blend of red and white) is the signature of incomplete dominance. In codominance, both colors would show separately (spots), not blend.

stem: If a mutation occurs only in somatic (body) cells, the individual may —

options.a: experience an increased risk of cancer
options.c: pass on these mutations to their offspring
options.d: develop entirely new DNA sequences in all cells

explanation: Somatic cell mutations affect only that individual and can cause uncontrolled cell growth (cancer). They are NOT passed to offspring — only gamete mutations are inherited. The mutation doesn't spread to all cells.

stem: The chances of developing cancer, diabetes, or sickle-cell anemia are higher if a family member has the disorder because they are —
explanation: These disorders have a genetic component — family members share genes. If a gene variant that increases disease risk runs in a family, relatives are more likely to inherit it. They are NOT passed by blood contact or infection.

stem: Which is an example of a genetically engineered organism?

options.b: A plant that received external DNA to produce natural insecticides
options.c: A plant with naturally occurring medicinal properties
options.d: A new variety created by cross-pollination

explanation: Genetic engineering = directly inserting DNA from one organism into another using technology. Grafting and cross-pollination are traditional breeding methods, not genetic engineering. Natural medicinal properties are not engineered.

stem: DNA that is derived from the DNA of two or more different species is called —
explanation: Recombinant DNA is artificially created by combining DNA from two or more different sources (often different species). A plasmid is the VEHICLE used to carry recombinant DNA, not the recombinant DNA itself.

stem: CRISPR technology allows scientists to —

options.a: view DNA under a light microscope
options.c: precisely edit DNA at a specific location in the genome
options.d: make millions of copies of a DNA segment

explanation: CRISPR is a gene-editing tool that precisely cuts DNA at a specific location, allowing scientists to delete, modify, or insert genes. Separating fragments = gel electrophoresis. Making copies = PCR.

stem: A benefit of genetically engineering bacteria to produce human insulin is that it —

options.a: eliminates the need for any medical treatment
options.b: provides a reliable, large-scale source of insulin for diabetics
options.d: makes bacteria immune to all diseases

explanation: Before genetic engineering, insulin was extracted from pig or cow pancreases in limited quantities. Engineered bacteria can produce unlimited human insulin, making it accessible to millions of diabetics worldwide.

stem: An x-ray diffraction image of DNA led to the idea that DNA —
explanation: Rosalind Franklin's x-ray diffraction photo showed a distinctive X pattern that indicated DNA has a helical (spiral) structure. This was critical evidence for Watson and Crick's double helix model.

stem: Which is most responsible for carrying coded information from the nucleus?
explanation: mRNA carries the genetic code from DNA in the nucleus to the ribosome in the cytoplasm for translation into protein.

stem: Tissue from heart and stomach of a grasshopper would have the same —
explanation: All cells in an organism contain the same DNA. Different tissues have different shapes, sizes, and metabolic rates, but the genetic content is identical.

stem: In a heterozygous cow (Ll), what % of gametes carry the dominant allele?
explanation: Meiosis separates alleles. Ll produces 50% L gametes and 50% l gametes.

stem: Decreased genetic diversity in wild cat populations leads to —
explanation: Less genetic diversity = entire population vulnerable to the same diseases. Diverse populations have varied immune defenses.

stem: Heterozygous yellow squash (Yy x Yy): possible offspring genotypes?
explanation: Yy x Yy gives 1 YY : 2 Yy : 1 yy. All three genotypes are possible.

stem: Heterozygous short-haired rabbits (Ff x Ff): chance of short fur?
explanation: Ff x Ff: 1 FF + 2 Ff + 1 ff. Short fur (F) is dominant: FF, Ff, Ff all show short = 3 out of 4.

stem: Cross TTPP x ttpp. The offspring are —
explanation: TTPP x ttpp = all TtPp. Both T and P are dominant, so ALL offspring are tall and purple.

stem: Heterozygous green corn (Gg x Gg): chance of albino (gg)?
explanation: Gg x Gg: 1 GG + 2 Gg + 1 gg. Albino = gg = 1 out of 4 (25%).

stem: Pink snapdragons (Rr x Rr) produce —
explanation: Incomplete dominance: Rr x Rr = 1 RR (red) : 2 Rr (pink) : 1 rr (white). 1:2:1 ratio.

stem: Albinism appearing in children of pigmented parents shows it is —

options.d: requiring both parents to be albinos

explanation: Both parents look normal but produce albino child = both are carriers (heterozygous). Trait only shows when homozygous recessive.

stem: For a mutation to pass to offspring, it must occur in a —
explanation: Only gamete mutations are inherited. Somatic mutations affect only that individual.

stem: Somatic cell mutations from radiation may cause —

options.d: entirely new DNA in all cells

explanation: Somatic mutations can cause uncontrolled cell growth (cancer) but are NOT passed to offspring and don't spread to all cells.

stem: Inbreeding in endangered species may lead to extinction by —
explanation: Inbreeding = related individuals sharing the same alleles = less diversity = less ability to adapt.

stem: A potential benefit of the Human Genome Project is —

options.c: classifying humans in the animal kingdom
options.d: finding the genes responsible for many diseases

explanation: Sequencing the human genome helps identify disease-related genes, enabling genetic testing and targeted therapies.

stem: Which is a genetically engineered organism?

options.b: A plant with external DNA for insecticide production
options.c: A plant with natural medicinal properties

explanation: Genetic engineering = inserting external DNA using technology. Grafting and cross-pollination are traditional methods.

stem: DNA from two or more species combined is called —
explanation: Recombinant DNA is artificially created by combining DNA from different species. Plasmids carry recombinant DNA but aren't the recombinant DNA itself.

stem: CRISPR allows scientists to —

options.c: precisely edit DNA at specific genome locations

explanation: CRISPR = precise gene editing (find-and-replace for DNA). Gel electrophoresis separates by size. PCR copies DNA.

stem: Cancer, diabetes, and sickle-cell risk increases with family history because they are —
explanation: These conditions have genetic components. Family members share genes, so disease-associated alleles run in families.

stem: A student hypothesizes thick leg muscles are inherited. Dogs outdoors have thicker muscles than indoor dogs. The student should conclude —

options.a: Inheritance alone may not account for thick leg muscles
options.b: Dogs with thick muscles can't survive indoors
options.d: Thick muscles are associated with coat thickness
